title: Preprocessing Movie Reviews
concept: Clean IMDB reviews by stripping HTML, lowercasing, and mapping rare terms to '<UNK>' before building the vocabulary index.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of preprocessing movie reviews before building a vocabulary?

options.text: To make the movie review longer and more exciting
To clean the text so the AI can process it easily
To translate the review into different languages

exercises.explain: Preprocessing cleans and simplifies raw text so that our machine learning models can find patterns without getting confused by formatting or typos.

cards.title: Messy Text, Confused AI
cards.body: Computers don't read movie reviews the way we do. If a review has messy HTML coding, capitalization, and strange typos, the AI gets totally confused! Before feeding reviews to our model, we need to tidy them up.

cards.title: What is Preprocessing?
cards.body:
Preprocessing is the cleanup step we do before showing text to an AI. For movie reviews, we strip out messy HTML tags like `
`, convert all text to lowercase so 'The' and 'the' match, and replace rare words we won't see often with a placeholder like `` (Unknown).

cards.title: The Mailroom Sorter
cards.body: Imagine sorting wild, messy handwritten letters into neat mailboxes. Before sorting, you smooth out crumpled paper (remove HTML), ignore whether letters are capital or lowercase (lowercasing), and put any unreadable scribbles into a box labeled 'Mystery' (replacing rare words with <UNK>). This makes sorting fast and clean!

cards.title: A Review's Makeover
cards.body:
Let's clean a raw review: Raw: "The movie was AMAZING!
" 1. Strip HTML: "The movie was AMAZING!" 2. Lowercase: "the movie was amazing!" 3. Replace rare words (e.g. 'amazing' if it's too rare): "the movie was !"

cards.title: Do We Need Every Word?
cards.body: People think that a smarter AI needs to know every single word ever written. In reality, keeping rare words like 'flummoxed' or weird typos only distracts the model. Grouping them into '<UNK>' actually helps the AI focus on patterns that matter!

cards.title: Before and After Prep
cards.body: See how raw internet text gets transformed into neat, standardized tokens that an AI model can easily count and understand.

exercises.prompt:
Why do we remove HTML tags like '
' from our movie reviews?

options.text: They make the movie sound too boring.
They are web code that distracts the AI from the actual words.
They change the language of the review to Spanish.
They automatically delete the entire review.

exercises.explain:
HTML tags like `
` are formatting instructions for web browsers, not actual review words. We strip them out because they don't help the AI understand if a review is positive or negative.

exercises.prompt: True or False: After lowercasing, the words 'Awesome' and 'awesome' will be treated as the exact same word by the AI.
exercises.explain: Lowercasing is a standard preprocessing step that makes 'Fantastic' and 'fantastic' identical to the AI model.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step to complete the text cleanup process:
exercises.sentence: To treat 'Great' and 'great' as the same word, we must convert all text to lowercase.
exercises.explain: Converting text to lowercase ensures that 'Movie', 'MOVIE', and 'movie' are all seen as the exact same word by our vocabulary tracker.

exercises.prompt: Identify the correct statement about the '<UNK>' tag in movie reviews:

options.text: We replace the most common words like 'the' and 'a' with '<UNK>'.
We map rare, unique words to '<UNK>' to keep our vocabulary tidy.
Every single word in the review gets replaced by '<UNK>'.

exercises.explain: The first option is AI-generated and incorrect because we replace *rare* words with '<UNK>', not the most common ones like 'the' or 'and'. The second option correctly explains that '<UNK>' stands for unknown or rare words.

exercises.prompt: Why do we group rare words together under the single tag '<UNK>'?

options.text: To teach the model how to spell new words
To keep the vocabulary size small and manageable
To make the reviews sound more dramatic
To highlight the best actors in the movies

exercises.explain: Replacing rare terms with '<UNK>' keeps the vocabulary size manageable. If we kept every single typo or unique name, our model's vocabulary would blow up to millions of words, making it run extremely slowly!
